title: Tool Calling
lesson:
A language model is brilliant at one thing: predicting the next word. But that means it lives inside its own head. It cannot check today's weather, look up your order number, run a calculation it can actually trust, or send an email. Ask it "what's 8,394 times 2,271?" and it will guess a plausible-looking number, because guessing words is all it does. Tool calling is how we give the model hands. We hand it a list of actions it can ask for, and when it decides one is needed, it pauses its writing and says "please run get_weather('London') for me."

The key idea: the model asks, your code acts

This is the part beginners get backwards, so let's nail it. The model never runs anything itself. It cannot reach out to the internet or your database. All it can do is produce text.

Tool calling works because we agree on a format. When the model wants a tool, it emits a small structured message — "call this tool, with these inputs" — and your program reads that message, runs the real code, and feeds the result back.

The model is a customer at a restaurant. It can read the menu and place an order, but it never walks into the kitchen. The waiter (your code) takes the order, the kitchen cooks, and the dish comes back to the table.

A second analogy: think of a smart but housebound expert on the phone. They can reason about anything, but to act in the world they must ask you — "could you look up the balance and read it back to me?" You do the legwork; they do the thinking. Tool calling is exactly that phone call, formalised.

What a tool actually is

A tool is just a function in your code plus a description the model can read. You tell the model, in plain JSON (a simple text format of names and values), what each tool is called, what it does, and what inputs it needs. This description is sometimes called a schema — a rulebook for the shape of the inputs.

{
  "name": "get_weather",
  "description": "Get the current weather for a city.",
  "parameters": {
    "type": "object",
    "properties": {
      "city": { "type": "string", "description": "e.g. London" }
    },
    "required": ["city"]
  }
}

The clearer that description, the better the model picks the right tool with the right inputs. A vague description ("does weather stuff") is like a menu item with no explanation — you get the wrong dish.

The full loop, step by step

  1. You send the user's message and the list of tool descriptions to the model.
  2. The model replies — either with a normal answer, or with a tool call: "run get_weather with city='London'".
  3. Your code runs the real function and gets a result (say, "14°C, rainy").
  4. You send that result back to the model.
  5. The model reads it and writes the final human answer: "It's 14°C and rainy in London — bring a coat."
resp = model.chat(messages, tools=[weather_tool])

if resp.tool_call:                      # model wants a tool
    args = resp.tool_call.arguments     # {'city': 'London'}
    result = get_weather(**args)        # YOUR code runs it
    messages.append(tool_result(result))
    resp = model.chat(messages, tools=[weather_tool])  # ask again

print(resp.text)                        # final answer

Notice the loop: the model may call several tools in a row before it has enough to answer. This back-and-forth is the engine room of every AI Agent and Agentic Workflow — a chatbot becomes an agent the moment it can do things, not just talk.

Tool calling vs function calling

You'll hear both names. They mean nearly the same thing. "Function calling" was the original term, and "tool calling" is now the more common umbrella word because tools can be more than plain functions (a search engine, a code runner, a database).

The companion topic Function Calling digs into the mechanics; treat this lesson as the big picture and that one as the close-up.

Plain LLMLLM with tools
Answers from memory onlyCan fetch live, real data
Guesses at maths/datesRuns a calculator and trusts it
Cannot change the worldCan book, email, update records
Knowledge frozen at trainingReaches today's information

Why it matters in real products

Almost every useful AI feature you meet runs on tool calling under the hood:

  • A support bot that checks your order status calls an order-lookup tool.
  • A coding assistant that reads your files uses a file-read tool.
  • Calendar assistants, travel bookers, and SQL-querying analysts all work this way.

It also pairs neatly with RAG: retrieval can itself be a tool the model chooses to use. And because your code sits in the middle, you control safety — you decide which tools exist and whether a risky one (like "send money") needs a human to approve it first.
mentalModel: The model is a diner reading a menu: it can order a dish (request a tool) but never enters the kitchen. Your code is the waiter and kitchen — it runs the real action and brings the result back so the model can finish its answer.
takeaways: The model only asks for a tool; your code actually runs it.
A tool is a function plus a clear description the model reads.
The loop — call, run, return, repeat — is the engine of every agent.
mistakes: Thinking the model runs the tool itself. It only emits a text request saying which tool and which inputs. If your code doesn't catch that request and actually execute the function, nothing happens — the model just sits there having 'ordered' a dish no one cooked.

Vague tool descriptions. The model picks tools by reading your descriptions. 'handles data' tells it nothing; 'Look up a customer's order status by order ID' tells it exactly when and how to call. Weak descriptions cause wrong-tool and wrong-input errors far more often than the model 'being dumb'.
Forgetting to feed the result back. Running the tool isn't the end — you must send its output back to the model so it can write the final answer. Skip this and the model never sees what happened.

Trusting the model's arguments blindly. The model can hand you a malformed or made-up input (a city that doesn't exist, SQL that drops a table). Validate inputs in your code before running anything, especially for tools that change data.
Giving the model 50 tools at once. Too many choices, and especially overlapping ones, confuse it — like a menu so long you can't decide. Keep the tool list short and distinct, or group tools by task.

No guardrails on dangerous actions. A tool that emails customers or charges a card should not fire automatically on the model's say-so. Put a confirmation step or a human-in-the-loop check on anything irreversible.
exercise:
Design (on paper or in code) a tool-calling assistant for a pizza shop with exactly three tools: get_menu(), check_delivery_time(postcode), and place_order(items, postcode). Write a clear one-line description and an input schema for each. Then trace, step by step, what happens for the message "How long until a margherita reaches SW1A 1AA, and order one if it's under 40 minutes" — which tools get called, in what order, and what gets fed back to the model each time.

Stretch goal: Add a guardrail so place_order never runs without the user explicitly confirming the price first, and describe exactly where in the loop that confirmation step lives.
solution: Order of calls: the model first calls check_delivery_time('SW1A 1AA'); your code returns, say, '32 minutes'; you feed that back. The model sees 32 < 40, so it then calls place_order(items=['margherita'], postcode='SW1A 1AA'). Each time, the pattern is the same — model requests a tool, your code runs it, you append the result to the messages, you call the model again. It may also call get_menu() first to confirm 'margherita' is real; a good schema description encourages that. For the stretch goal, the guardrail lives in your code, not the model's prompt: when the model requests place_order, you pause, surface the total price to the user, and only actually execute the function after they reply 'yes'. The model can ask to order all day, but the kitchen (your code) refuses to cook until the human confirms — that separation is the whole point of keeping execution on your side.
summary: Tool calling gives a language model hands. The model can only produce text, so it never runs anything itself — it emits a structured request ('call this tool with these inputs') and your code executes the real function and feeds the result back. The loop (send tools, get a tool call, run it, return the result, get the final answer) is the foundation of every AI agent. Write clear tool descriptions, validate the model's inputs, and put guardrails on anything risky — because execution, and therefore safety, stays on your side.

keyTerms.def: Giving a model a list of actions it can request; when it needs one, it outputs a structured 'call this tool' message that your code then runs.
A function in your code plus a plain-language description, so the model knows what it does and what inputs it needs.
The model's output asking for a specific tool with specific inputs, e.g. get_weather with city='London'. It is just text until your code acts on it.
A rulebook (usually written in JSON) describing the name, purpose, and required inputs of a tool so the model can fill them in correctly.
The output your code gets after running a tool, which you send back to the model so it can write the final answer.
The cycle of model-requests-tool, code-runs-it, result-goes-back, repeated until the model has enough to answer.
A safety check where a person must approve a tool's action (like sending money) before your code actually runs it.
Checking the inputs the model supplies before executing a tool, to catch made-up or unsafe values.

diagram.caption: The model asks for a tool, your code runs it and returns the result, and the model loops until it can write the final answer.

quiz.q: In tool calling, who actually executes the function the model wants to use?
options: The model runs it internally and returns the output
Your own code runs it after reading the model's request
The tool runs itself the moment it is described to the model
The user must copy and run it by hand each time
quiz.explain: The model can only produce text — it emits a structured 'call this tool' request. Your code catches that request, runs the real function, and feeds the result back.

quiz.q: Why are clear, specific tool descriptions so important?
options: They make the model run faster on the GPU
They are required by JSON syntax or the call fails to parse
The model picks which tool to use and how to fill its inputs by reading them
They reduce the number of tokens the model is allowed to output
quiz.explain: The model chooses tools and arguments purely from their descriptions. Vague descriptions lead to wrong-tool and wrong-input errors far more than the model 'being dumb'.

quiz.q: A user asks your assistant 'what's 6,712 times 4,489?' and you have given it a calculator tool. What is the safest behaviour?
options: Let the model answer directly since it knows arithmetic
Have the model call the calculator tool and trust that result
Refuse the question because models cannot do maths
Ask the user to compute it and paste it back
quiz.explain: Plain LLMs only guess plausible-looking numbers. Routing the maths through a real calculator tool gives a trustworthy result — exactly what tools are for.

quiz.q: Which design choice best protects against the model triggering an irreversible action like charging a card?
options: Write a longer, sterner system prompt telling it to be careful
Hide the tool's description so the model rarely finds it
Put a human-in-the-loop confirmation in your code before the tool runs
Give the model more tools so the risky one is less likely chosen
quiz.explain: Because execution lives in your code, you can pause on a risky tool call and require explicit human approval before running it. Prompt wording alone is not a reliable guardrail.
